_meta.description: KNOWN-FALSE attribution ground truth for the Provenance Delta benchmark. Each row is a lineage merge that is documented as false. The LABEL comes from the cited external source + reason, NOT from Sophia's gate. Authorship-scoped to match what the gate adjudicates.

misattributions.reason: The Dao De Jing is a Daoist text traditionally ascribed to Laozi; Confucius belongs to a distinct (Confucian) tradition and is never credited with it.
Socrates is a character/speaker in the dialogue; Plato is the author. Socrates wrote nothing.
A Greek philosopher cannot be the author of a Chinese Daoist classic; different tradition and era.
The Analects records Confucius; Laozi is the Daoist figure, a different lineage.
The Mencius records Mencius, who lived generations after Confucius; same tradition but not the same author.
The Republic is Plato's; Aristotle was his student and wrote separate works (e.g. the Nicomachean Ethics, Politics).
Meditations is by Marcus Aurelius. Epictetus is associated with the Enchiridion (compiled by Arrian).
The Enchiridion derives from Epictetus via Arrian; Marcus Aurelius wrote the Meditations, a different Stoic work.
The Manifesto is by Marx and Engels (1848); Nietzsche was a different thinker with no role in it.
Beyond Good and Evil is Nietzsche's; Freud was a later psychoanalyst, not its author.
The Prince is Machiavelli's (1513); Kant is an 18th-century German philosopher.
Though both are Daoist, the Dao De Jing is ascribed to Laozi and the Zhuangzi to Zhuang Zhou; conflating them merges two distinct texts/authors.
Traditionally bundled with Aristotle's corpus and ascribed to him until the Renaissance, but since the 16th c. attributed to Anaximenes of Lampsacus.
A pseudo-Platonic appendix to the Laws; scholarly consensus (following Diogenes Laertius) attributes it to Philip of Opus, not Plato.

misattributions.gold_author: an anonymous author ('the Old Oligarch' / Pseudo-Xenophon)
misattributions.reason: Preserved among Xenophon's works but now taken not to be his; the unknown author is called Pseudo-Xenophon or the Old Oligarch.

misattributions.work: the eighth book of the Gallic War
misattributions.reason: Caesar wrote Books 1-7; the eighth commentary was added after his death by Aulus Hirtius.
De Mundo (On the Universe) is regarded as spurious — a Pseudo-Aristotelian work not by Aristotle himself.

misattributions.work: the Ten Wings of the I Ching
misattributions.reason: The Ten Wings (commentaries on the I Ching) are traditionally credited to Confucius, but modern scholarship holds they were composed by later, anonymous hands.
The Donation of Constantine is a forged Roman imperial decree; Lorenzo Valla demonstrated it was fabricated centuries after Constantine.
Long included in the Corpus Aristotelicum but recognized as falsely attributed; the work derives from Nicolaus of Damascus.
A compilation accreted by Aristotle's school over centuries, not authored by Aristotle himself.
A mock epic the Romans ascribed to Homer; Plutarch credits Pigres, and language dates it long after Homer.
Called 'Homeric' for their epic style and meter, but not composed by Homer.
Casaubon (1614) showed the texts are Hellenistic Greek, not works of an ancient Egyptian sage.
A dialogue transmitted under Plato's name but regarded as spurious.
Alcibiades II is generally judged spurious, not by Plato.
Richard Bentley famously proved these letters a much later forgery, not by the tyrant Phalaris.
Attributed to Paul's 1st-century convert but actually written centuries later by an unknown Neoplatonist.
Part of the Caesarian corpus written by a continuator, not by Caesar himself.
A collection ascribed to the young Virgil but largely considered not his.
Pythagoras left no writings; the Golden Verses are a later Pythagorean composition.
The collection accreted over centuries; Aesop may be semi-legendary and did not author the corpus as transmitted.
Mosaic authorship of the Pentateuch is rejected by mainstream scholarship (documentary/supplementary hypotheses).
Traditionally ascribed to Solomon, but its language dates it centuries after him.
A Greek pseudepigraphon written under Solomon's name centuries after Solomon.

misattributions.work: chapters 40 to 66 of the Book of Isaiah
misattributions.reason: Scholarly consensus assigns Isaiah 40-66 to one or more later authors, not the 8th-c. Isaiah of Jerusalem.
Mainstream scholarship dates Daniel to the Maccabean era, not the Babylonian exile.
The Psalter is an anthology by numerous hands; 'David wrote the Psalms' is an oversimplified tradition.
Hebrews is formally anonymous; near-universal scholarship rejects Pauline authorship.
The Pastoral Epistles are widely regarded as pseudonymous, written after Paul's lifetime.
Titus is one of the Pastoral Epistles, majority-judged pseudonymous.
1 Enoch is a pseudepigraphon composed long after the antediluvian figure it names.
A pseudonymous sayings gospel; the apostle Thomas is not its author.

misattributions.gold_author: a later compiler (received text ~4th century CE)
misattributions.reason: The transmitted Liezi is generally held to be a much later compilation, not the work of the semi-legendary Lie Yukou.
Named for Guan Zhong but compiled over centuries after his death.

misattributions.gold_author: the state chroniclers of Lu (traditional Confucian authorship disputed)
misattributions.reason: Tradition credits Confucius as compiler/author, but modern scholarship doubts his direct authorship.
